content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a game on a binary string SSS of length NNN.

Alice and Bob alternate turns, with Alice going first. On their turn, the current player must choose any index 1≤i<∣S∣1 \le i \lt |S|1≤i<∣S∣ such that Si≠Si+1S_i \neq S_{i+1}Si​=Si+1​ and erase either SiS_iSi​ or Si+1S_{i+1}Si+1​ from the string. This reduces the length of SSS by one, and the remaining parts of SSS are concatenated in the same order.

The player who is unable to make a move loses.

Determine the winner if both players play optimally.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of the string.
The second line contains the binary string SSS of length NNN.


Output Format
For each test case output AliceAliceAlice if Alice wins the game, otherwise output BobBobBob.
The output is not case-sensitive, so for example "Bob", "boB" and "Bob" are all considered equivalent.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
S consists only of characters '0' and '1'
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N (the length of the string), the second line contains a binary string S of length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Alice" if Alice wins with optimal play, otherwise output "Bob".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: string is uniform, no move, Bob wins.\nTest case 2: zeros count = 1 (odd), Alice can delete the single zero and wins.\nTest case 3: both counts are even, any sequence of deletions has even length, Bob wins.

solution.approach: If the string is already uniform, no move is possible and Alice loses. Otherwise the players can always delete all characters of one type (0s or 1s) because each such character is adjacent to a character of the opposite type until that type disappears. The total number of moves equals the number of deleted characters, i.e., either the number of zeros or the number of ones. Alice moves first, so she wins iff she can force an odd total number of moves. Hence, if either count of zeros or count of ones is odd, Alice can choose to delete that type and wins; if both counts are even, any possible total number of moves is even and Bob wins.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the string.
